So most US companies approach it by filing their US application first, and then there's a treaty called the Paris Convention that allows you, if you file in a foreign country within six months, and that country is also a member of the Paris Convention, then you get the same filing date as your US filing date.
So you get the same priority.
Now, you don't have to do that.
You can always file directly in any country where you have plans to use the mark.
The US requires use before it will give you a registration.
Most foreign countries let you file an application and get a registration even without using the mark in that country.
And then what happens in most countries is after three years, if you haven't begun use, your mark becomes open to non-use cancellation.
